rawText:
One of the most useful skills in any workplace or classroom is the ability to explain a problem so that someone else can understand it quickly.

Many people struggle with this — not because they do not understand the problem themselves, but because they include too many details or start in the wrong place.

A clear explanation usually begins with a short statement of what the problem is, not how it was discovered or why it matters.

For example, instead of saying "Last week I was working on the report and noticed something strange in the numbers," it is better to start with "The sales figures for March are thirty percent lower than expected."

Once the main point is stated, the next step is to describe the context — when the problem started, what was expected to happen, and what actually happened instead.

Keeping these three elements in order — the problem, the expectation, and the reality — gives the listener a clear framework to follow.

After that, it helps to mention what you have already tried, so that the other person does not suggest solutions you have already ruled out.

A sentence like "I checked the data source and confirmed the inputs are correct, so the issue seems to be in the calculation step" saves time and shows that you have done your homework.

Avoid using vague language such as "something is wrong" or "it does not work," because those phrases force the listener to ask follow-up questions before they can even begin thinking about a solution.

Instead, be as specific as possible: name the system, the step, the number, or the behavior that is not matching what you expected.

Visual aids — a screenshot, a simple diagram, or even a quick sketch on paper — can often communicate in seconds what words take minutes to explain.

Finally, end with a clear question or request: "Can you check whether the formula changed?" is far more helpful than "What do you think?"

The goal is not to sound impressive but to make it as easy as possible for the other person to help you.

Good communicators are not the ones who use the most words — they are the ones who use the fewest words to get the right idea across.

sentences.text: When you have a problem, it is important to explain it clearly to others.
sentences.translation: 문제가 있을 때 다른 사람에게 잘 설명하는 것이 중요해요.

sentences.text: Many people say too much or start talking about the wrong thing first.
sentences.translation: 많은 사람들이 너무 많이 말하거나, 엉뚱한 것부터 이야기해요.

sentences.text: You should say what the problem is first, then explain what happened.
sentences.translation: 문제가 뭔지 먼저 말하고, 그다음에 무슨 일이 있었는지 설명해야 해요.

sentences.text: Tell the other person what you already tried so they do not repeat it.
sentences.translation: 이미 시도해 본 것을 상대방에게 말해야 같은 걸 반복하지 않아요.

sentences.text: Use exact words, not things like "something is wrong."
sentences.translation: "뭔가 이상해"같은 말 대신 정확한 말을 써야 해요.

sentences.text: Pictures or drawings can help explain things faster than words.
sentences.translation: 그림이나 사진이 말보다 더 빨리 설명하는 데 도움이 돼요.

sentences.text: The article explains how to describe a problem clearly so that the listener can help quickly.
sentences.translation: 이 글은 듣는 사람이 빨리 도울 수 있도록 문제를 명확히 설명하는 방법을 알려 줘요.

sentences.text: Most people fail not because they lack understanding but because they organize their explanation poorly.
sentences.translation: 대부분의 사람들이 실패하는 이유는 이해력이 부족해서가 아니라 설명을 잘 정리하지 못해서예요.

sentences.text: A good explanation follows a sequence: state the problem, give context, then describe what you have tried.
sentences.translation: 좋은 설명은 순서를 따라요: 문제를 말하고, 맥락을 주고, 시도한 것을 설명해요.

sentences.text: Vague language wastes time because the listener has to ask extra questions before helping.
sentences.translation: 모호한 표현은 시간을 낭비해요. 듣는 사람이 도와주기 전에 추가 질문을 해야 하니까요.

sentences.text: Visual aids like screenshots or diagrams can communicate complex information faster than words alone.
sentences.translation: 스크린샷이나 다이어그램 같은 시각 자료는 말만으로 할 때보다 복잡한 정보를 더 빨리 전달할 수 있어요.

sentences.text: The writer concludes that good communicators use fewer words to deliver the right message, not more.
sentences.translation: 글쓴이는 좋은 소통자는 더 많은 말이 아니라 더 적은 말로 올바른 메시지를 전달한다고 결론지어요.

sentences.text: This instructional article uses a sequential structure to teach the reader how to explain problems effectively in professional settings.
sentences.translation: 이 설명문은 순차적 구조를 사용해 독자에게 전문적 환경에서 문제를 효과적으로 설명하는 법을 가르쳐요.

sentences.text: The writer establishes the common failure mode first — excessive detail and poor ordering — before presenting the correct approach.
sentences.translation: 글쓴이는 올바른 접근법을 제시하기 전에 먼저 흔한 실패 유형 — 과도한 세부사항과 잘못된 순서 —을 제시해요.

sentences.text: The three-part framework — problem, expectation, reality — mirrors classic problem-definition models used in engineering and consulting.
sentences.translation: 문제, 기대, 현실이라는 세 부분의 프레임워크는 엔지니어링과 컨설팅에서 쓰이는 고전적 문제 정의 모델을 반영해요.

sentences.text: Concrete examples — contrasting a vague opening with a direct statement — make the advice immediately actionable for the reader.
sentences.translation: 구체적 예시 — 모호한 도입부와 직접적 진술을 대비한 것 —은 독자가 조언을 즉시 실행할 수 있게 해 줘요.

sentences.text: The rhetorical strategy shifts from "what to do" to "what to avoid," covering both prescriptive and proscriptive dimensions of clear communication.
sentences.translation: 수사적 전략이 "무엇을 해야 하는지"에서 "무엇을 피해야 하는지"로 전환되어, 명확한 소통의 처방적·금지적 차원을 모두 다뤄요.

sentences.text: The closing aphorism — fewest words, right idea — encapsulates the article's thesis and gives readers a memorable takeaway.
sentences.translation: 마무리 격언 — 가장 적은 말, 올바른 아이디어 —은 글 전체의 논지를 함축하며 독자에게 기억에 남는 교훈을 줘요.

sentences.original: One of the most useful skills in any workplace or classroom is the ability to explain a problem so that someone else can understand it quickly.
sentences.translation: 어떤 직장이나 교실에서든 가장 유용한 기술 중 하나는, 다른 사람이 빠르게 이해할 수 있도록 문제를 설명하는 능력이다.

grammarElements.text: One of the most useful skills
grammarElements.label: 주어 (one of + 최상급)

grammarElements.text: the ability to explain a problem
grammarElements.label: 보어 (to 부정사 수식)

grammarElements.text: so that someone else can understand it quickly
grammarElements.label: 목적절 (so that)

vocabulary.example: This is a useful skill for any job.
She has the ability to solve problems quickly.
Can you explain the problem to me?

expressions.usage: 최상급을 완화하면서 강조할 때 쓰는 표현. 단수 동사(is)가 따른다
목적이나 의도를 나타내는 접속사. 결과를 명시할 때 쓴다

patterns.pattern: One of the most [adjective] [noun] is the ability to [verb] so that [result]
patterns.explanation: 특정 능력의 중요성을 강조하며 글을 시작하는 도입 패턴. so that으로 그 능력의 목적까지 명시한다.
examples: One of the most important skills is the ability to listen so that others feel understood.
One of the most valuable habits is the ability to plan ahead so that deadlines are met.

drillQuestions.question: One of the most useful skills _____ the ability to explain clearly.
You explain a problem so _____ someone else can understand.
"One of the most useful skills" uses the _____ form.
The ability to explain is important in any workplace or _____.

sentences.notes: 글 전체의 주제를 도입하는 문장. "One of the most useful skills"로 독자의 관심을 끌고, so that절로 명확한 설명이 왜 중요한지 목적을 바로 밝힌다.

koreanAlignment.en: One of the most useful skills
in any workplace or classroom
is the ability to explain a problem
so that someone else can understand it quickly
koreanAlignment.ko: 다른 사람이 빠르게 이해할 수 있도록

pronunciationNotes: ability: /əˈbɪləti/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "어빌리티"에 가깝다.
explain: /ɪkˈspleɪn/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. ex-를 /ɪks/로 빠르게 발음한다.

sentences.original: Many people struggle with this — not because they do not understand the problem themselves, but because they include too many details or start in the wrong place.
sentences.translation: 많은 사람이 이것을 어려워한다 — 스스로 문제를 이해하지 못해서가 아니라, 너무 많은 세부사항을 포함하거나 잘못된 곳에서 시작하기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: not because they do not understand the problem themselves
grammarElements.label: 부정 이유절

grammarElements.text: but because they include too many details or start in the wrong place
grammarElements.label: 대조 이유절 (진짜 이유)

vocabulary.example: Many people struggle with public speaking.
Do not include too many details at once.
The report includes several charts.

expressions.usage: 어떤 일이 쉽지 않음을 표현할 때 쓴다. with 뒤에 어려움의 대상이 온다

expressions.phrase: not because A, but because B
expressions.usage: 진짜 이유를 강조하기 위해 거짓 이유를 먼저 부정하는 구문

patterns.pattern: [Subject] struggle with [noun] — not because [false reason], but because [real reason]
patterns.explanation: 대시(—) 뒤에 "not because ~ but because ~" 구조로 흔한 오해를 깨고 진짜 원인을 밝히는 패턴.
examples: Students struggle with math — not because it is impossible, but because they skip the basics.
Teams struggle with deadlines — not because they lack talent, but because they plan poorly.

drillQuestions.question: Many people _____ with explaining problems clearly.
They fail not because they do not understand, _____ because they include too many details.
"Start in the wrong place" means beginning the explanation with the wrong _____.
"Too many details" is a problem of giving _____ information.

sentences.notes: "not because A, but because B" 구조가 흔한 오해(이해력 부족)를 깨고 진짜 원인(정보 과잉, 잘못된 순서)을 밝힌다. 대시(—)가 부연 설명의 시작을 시각적으로 구분한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Many people struggle with this
not because they do not understand the problem themselves
but because they include too many details
or start in the wrong place

pronunciationNotes: struggle: /ˈstrʌɡl/ — str을 한 번에 빠르게 발음. "스트러글"에 가깝다.
details: /ˈdiːteɪlz/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "디테일즈"에 가깝다. 미국식에서는 /dɪˈteɪlz/도 가능.

sentences.original: A clear explanation usually begins with a short statement of what the problem is, not how it was discovered or why it matters.
sentences.translation: 명확한 설명은 대개 문제가 무엇인지에 대한 짧은 진술로 시작한다 — 어떻게 발견되었는지나 왜 중요한지가 아니라.

grammarElements.text: a short statement of what the problem is
grammarElements.label: 전치사 목적어 (간접의문문 포함)

grammarElements.text: not how it was discovered or why it matters
grammarElements.label: 부정 대조구 (간접의문문 병렬)

vocabulary.example: A good explanation is short and clear.
Start with a short statement of the issue.
The error was discovered last week.

expressions.usage: 어떤 과정이나 구조의 출발점을 설명할 때 쓰는 표현
불필요한 요소를 부정하여 핵심 요소를 부각할 때 쓴다

patterns.pattern: [Subject] usually begins with [what to include], not [what to exclude]
patterns.explanation: 올바른 출발점과 잘못된 출발점을 대비하여 실천적 조언을 전달하는 패턴.
examples: A good essay usually begins with a thesis, not a personal anecdote.
A job interview usually begins with your qualifications, not your salary expectations.

drillQuestions.question: A clear explanation usually _____ with a short statement.
Start with what the problem _____, not how it was found.
"A short statement" means a _____ description.
"How it was discovered" uses the _____ voice.

sentences.notes: 설명의 첫 단계를 제시하는 핵심 조언 문장. "what the problem is"라는 간접의문문과 "not how/why"의 대조로, 무엇을 먼저 말해야 하는지 명확히 가르친다.

koreanAlignment.en: A clear explanation
usually begins with
a short statement of what the problem is
not how it was discovered or why it matters

pronunciationNotes: explanation: /ˌekspləˈneɪʃən/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "엑스플러네이션"에 가깝다.
statement: /ˈsteɪtmənt/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "스테이트먼트"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: For example, instead of saying "Last week I was working on the report and noticed something strange in the numbers," it is better to start with "The sales figures for March are thirty percent lower than expected."
sentences.translation: 예를 들어, "지난주에 보고서를 작성하다가 숫자에서 이상한 점을 발견했어요"라고 말하는 대신, "3월 매출 수치가 예상보다 30퍼센트 낮습니다"로 시작하는 것이 더 낫다.

grammarElements.text: "Last week I was working on the report and noticed something strange in the numbers"
grammarElements.label: 인용문 (나쁜 예)

grammarElements.text: it is better to start with
grammarElements.label: 가주어 it 구문 (비교급)

grammarElements.text: "The sales figures for March are thirty percent lower than expected"
grammarElements.label: 인용문 (좋은 예)

vocabulary.example: Instead of complaining, offer a solution.
The sales figures look unusual this month.
The results were lower than expected.

expressions.usage: 나쁜 표현과 좋은 표현을 대비할 때 쓰는 구문
더 나은 방법을 권유할 때 쓰는 가주어 구문

patterns.pattern: Instead of [vague approach], it is better to [specific approach]
patterns.explanation: 모호한 방식과 구체적 방식을 대비하여 실용적 조언을 제공하는 패턴. 실제 예문을 인용해 이해를 돕는다.
examples: Instead of saying "the project has issues," it is better to say "the deadline will be missed by two weeks."
Instead of writing "things went wrong," it is better to write "the server crashed at 3 PM."

drillQuestions.question: _____ of saying something vague, be specific.
It is better _____ start with the main point.
"Sales figures" refers to _____ data.
"Thirty percent lower than expected" is a _____ statement.

sentences.notes: 두 인용문을 대비하여 "나쁜 예"와 "좋은 예"를 직접 보여 주는 구체적 예시 문장. "instead of ~ it is better to ~" 구문이 독자에게 즉시 실행 가능한 조언을 준다.

koreanAlignment.en: For example, instead of saying
"Last week I was working on the report and noticed something strange in the numbers"
koreanAlignment.ko: "지난주에 보고서를 작성하다가 숫자에서 이상한 점을 발견했어요"

koreanAlignment.en: it is better to start with
"The sales figures for March are thirty percent lower than expected"

pronunciationNotes: figures: /ˈfɪɡjərz/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "피겨즈"에 가깝다. gu를 /ɡj/로 발음한다.
percent: /pərˈsent/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "퍼센트"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Once the main point is stated, the next step is to describe the context — when the problem started, what was expected to happen, and what actually happened instead.
sentences.translation: 핵심 요점이 진술되면, 다음 단계는 맥락을 설명하는 것이다 — 문제가 언제 시작되었는지, 무엇이 일어나기로 기대되었는지, 그리고 실제로는 무엇이 대신 일어났는지.

grammarElements.text: Once the main point is stated
grammarElements.label: 시간절 (Once + 수동태)

grammarElements.text: when the problem started, what was expected to happen, and what actually happened instead
grammarElements.label: 대시 뒤 간접의문문 3개 병렬

vocabulary.example: You need to provide more context for the reader.
The main point has been clearly stated.
What actually happened was quite different.

expressions.usage: Once가 "일단 ~하면"의 의미로, 선행 조건 완료 후 다음 단계를 안내할 때 쓴다
순서대로 과정을 안내하는 설명문에서 자주 쓰는 순차 표현

patterns.pattern: Once [step A is done], the next step is to [step B] — [element 1], [element 2], and [element 3]
patterns.explanation: 순차적 과정을 안내하며, 대시 뒤에 구체적 구성 요소를 병렬로 나열하는 패턴.
examples: Once the topic is chosen, the next step is to outline the structure — introduction, body, and conclusion.
Once the data is collected, the next step is to analyze it — trends, outliers, and patterns.

drillQuestions.question: _____ the main point is stated, describe the context.
The next step is to describe the _____.
The three elements are: when it started, what was expected, and what actually _____.
"The main point is stated" uses the _____ voice.

sentences.notes: 설명의 두 번째 단계를 순서대로 안내하는 문장. "Once ~ the next step is to ~"로 순차적 프로세스를 명확히 하고, 대시 뒤 세 가지 간접의문문이 맥락의 구성 요소를 정리한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Once the main point is stated
the next step is to describe the context
when the problem started
what was expected to happen
and what actually happened instead

pronunciationNotes: context: /ˈkɑːntekst/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "칸텍스트"에 가깝다.
actually: /ˈæktʃuəli/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "액추얼리"에 가깝다. -tually를 빠르게 발음한다.

sentences.original: Keeping these three elements in order — the problem, the expectation, and the reality — gives the listener a clear framework to follow.
sentences.translation: 이 세 가지 요소를 순서대로 유지하면 — 문제, 기대, 현실 — 듣는 사람에게 따라갈 수 있는 명확한 틀을 제공한다.

grammarElements.text: Keeping these three elements in order
grammarElements.label: 주어 (동명사구)

grammarElements.text: the problem, the expectation, and the reality
grammarElements.label: 대시 삽입 동격어구

vocabulary.example: These are the key elements of a good presentation.
The result did not match the expectation.
A clear framework helps organize your thoughts.

expressions.usage: 정해진 순서를 유지해야 한다는 의미. "in order to"(~하기 위해)와 구분해야 한다
구조화된 사고 방식을 제공한다는 의미. to follow가 framework을 수식한다

patterns.pattern: Keeping [elements] in order — [A], [B], and [C] — gives [person] a clear [noun] to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 동명사 주어로 핵심 원칙을 제시하고, 대시 삽입으로 구체적 요소를 열거한 뒤 결과를 밝히는 패턴.
examples: Keeping the steps in order — preparation, execution, and review — gives the team a clear process to trust.
Keeping the sections in order — introduction, analysis, and conclusion — gives the reader a clear path to follow.

drillQuestions.question: _____ these three elements in order gives the listener a framework.
The three elements are: the problem, the expectation, and the _____.
"Keeping" at the start of a sentence is a _____ acting as the subject.
A clear framework helps the listener to _____ the explanation.

sentences.notes: 핵심 프레임워크(문제-기대-현실)를 요약하는 문장. 동명사 주어(Keeping ~)가 행동 원칙을, 대시 삽입이 세 요소를 시각적으로 정리하며, "framework"이라는 단어로 체계적 접근을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Keeping these three elements in order
koreanAlignment.ko: 이 세 가지 요소를 순서대로 유지하면

koreanAlignment.en: the problem, the expectation, and the reality
gives the listener
a clear framework to follow

pronunciationNotes: expectation: /ˌekspekˈteɪʃən/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "엑스펙테이션"에 가깝다.
framework: /ˈfreɪmwɜːrk/ — 첫 음절에 강세. 합성어로 "프레임워크"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: After that, it helps to mention what you have already tried, so that the other person does not suggest solutions you have already ruled out.
sentences.translation: 그다음에는, 이미 시도해 본 것을 언급하는 것이 도움이 된다 — 상대방이 이미 배제한 해결책을 제안하지 않도록.

grammarElements.text: so that the other person does not suggest solutions
grammarElements.label: 목적절 (so that + 부정)

vocabulary.example: It helps to mention your previous attempts.
Do not suggest ideas that have been tried.
We need to find practical solutions.

expressions.usage: 순서를 나타내는 연결 표현. 이전 단계가 끝난 후를 가리킨다
가능성이나 선택지를 검토 후 제거했음을 나타내는 구동사

patterns.pattern: After that, it helps to [verb] what you have already [done], so that [person] does not [unwanted action]
patterns.explanation: 순서의 다음 단계를 안내하면서, so that절로 그 행동의 실용적 목적을 밝히는 패턴.
examples: After that, it helps to list what you have already checked, so that the technician does not repeat the same tests.
After that, it helps to share what you have already read, so that the advisor does not recommend the same sources.

drillQuestions.question: After that, it _____ to mention what you have tried.
Mention what you have tried so that others do not suggest solutions you have already _____.
"What you have already tried" is a _____ clause.
"So that" introduces a clause of _____.

sentences.notes: 설명의 세 번째 단계를 안내하는 문장. "After that"으로 순서를 이어 가고, "ruled out"이라는 구동사가 이미 검토하고 제외한 해결책을 간결히 표현한다.

koreanAlignment.en: After that
it helps to mention
what you have already tried
so that the other person does not suggest
solutions you have already ruled out

pronunciationNotes: mention: /ˈmenʃən/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "멘션"에 가깝다. -tion이 /ʃən/으로 발음된다.
solutions: /səˈluːʃənz/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "솔루션즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: A sentence like "I checked the data source and confirmed the inputs are correct, so the issue seems to be in the calculation step" saves time and shows that you have done your homework.
sentences.translation: "데이터 출처를 확인하고 입력값이 맞는 것을 확인했으므로, 문제는 계산 단계에 있는 것 같습니다"와 같은 문장은 시간을 절약하고, 당신이 사전 준비를 했음을 보여 준다.

grammarElements.text: that you have done your homework
grammarElements.label: that 명사절

vocabulary.example: I confirmed that the data is correct.
There might be an error in the calculation step.
The issue seems to be in the formula.

expressions.usage: 효율적인 행동이 불필요한 시간 소모를 줄여 준다는 의미

expressions.meaning: 사전 준비를 하다, 공부를 해 오다
expressions.usage: 숙제라는 본래 의미에서 확장되어, 미리 조사하고 준비했다는 관용 표현

patterns.pattern: A sentence like "[example]" saves time and shows that [positive outcome]
patterns.explanation: 구체적인 예문을 보여 준 뒤, 그것이 가져오는 실질적 이점 두 가지를 병렬로 제시하는 패턴.
examples: A sentence like "I tested three options and option B worked best" saves time and shows that you thought carefully.
A sentence like "I reviewed the logs and found no errors" saves time and shows that you are thorough.

drillQuestions.question: A clear sentence like this _____ time and shows preparation.
"Done your homework" means you have _____ the issue beforehand.
"The issue seems to be in the calculation step" is a _____ statement.
"Confirmed the inputs are correct" uses a _____ clause after confirmed.

sentences.notes: 예문을 직접 인용하여 좋은 설명의 구체적 모습을 보여 준다. "done your homework"라는 관용 표현이 사전 준비의 중요성을 강조하며, saves와 shows 두 동사의 병렬이 이점을 명확히 한다.

koreanAlignment.en: A sentence like
"I checked the data source and confirmed the inputs are correct, so the issue seems to be in the calculation step"
koreanAlignment.ko: "데이터 출처를 확인하고 입력값이 맞는 것을 확인했으므로, 문제는 계산 단계에 있는 것 같습니다"

koreanAlignment.en: saves time
and shows that you have done your homework
koreanAlignment.ko: 당신이 사전 준비를 했음을 보여 준다

pronunciationNotes: calculation: /ˌkælkjəˈleɪʃən/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "캘큘레이션"에 가깝다.
homework: /ˈhoʊmwɜːrk/ — 첫 음절에 강세. 합성어로 "홈워크"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Avoid using vague language such as "something is wrong" or "it does not work," because those phrases force the listener to ask follow-up questions before they can even begin thinking about a solution.
sentences.translation: "뭔가 잘못됐어요"나 "작동하지 않아요"같은 모호한 표현을 사용하지 마라 — 그런 표현은 듣는 사람이 해결책을 생각하기도 전에 후속 질문을 하도록 강제하기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: such as "something is wrong" or "it does not work"
grammarElements.label: 예시구 (such as)

grammarElements.text: because those phrases force the listener to ask follow-up questions
grammarElements.label: 이유절 (because + force + to 부정사)

grammarElements.text: before they can even begin thinking about a solution
grammarElements.label: 시간절 (before)

vocabulary.example: Avoid vague descriptions when reporting a problem.
Unclear messages force people to ask more questions.
She asked several follow-up questions.

expressions.usage: 구체적인 예를 나열할 때 쓰는 표현. 문어체에서 "like"보다 격식적
even이 "~조차"의 의미로, 아직 그 단계에도 이르지 못했음을 강조

patterns.explanation: 금지 조언을 제시하고 because절로 그 이유를 설명하는 패턴. 설명문에서 "하지 말아야 할 것"을 가르칠 때 쓴다.
examples: Avoid using technical jargon, because the audience may not understand it.
Avoid sending long emails, because busy readers will skip most of the content.

drillQuestions.question: _____ using vague language when explaining a problem.
"Something is wrong" is an example of _____ language.
Vague phrases force the listener to ask _____ questions.
They cannot even _____ thinking about a solution.

sentences.notes: 금지 조언(Avoid ~)으로 전환하며, 모호한 표현의 실질적 해악을 because절로 설명한다. "even begin thinking"이 모호한 표현이 초래하는 지연의 심각성을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Avoid using vague language
such as "something is wrong" or "it does not work"
because those phrases force the listener
koreanAlignment.ko: 그런 표현은 듣는 사람이 ~하도록 강제하기 때문이다

koreanAlignment.en: to ask follow-up questions
before they can even begin thinking about a solution

pronunciationNotes: vague: /veɪɡ/ — 한 음절. "베이그"에 가깝다. v를 확실히 발음한다.
follow-up: /ˈfɑːloʊ ʌp/ — 첫 음절에 강세. 하이픈으로 연결된 합성 형용사.

sentences.original: Instead, be as specific as possible: name the system, the step, the number, or the behavior that is not matching what you expected.
sentences.translation: 대신, 가능한 한 구체적이어라: 시스템, 단계, 숫자, 또는 예상과 맞지 않는 동작의 이름을 말하라.

grammarElements.text: name the system, the step, the number, or the behavior
grammarElements.label: 명령문 (병렬 목적어)

grammarElements.text: that is not matching what you expected
grammarElements.label: 관계절 + 간접의문문

vocabulary.example: Please be more specific about the error.
Describe the unexpected behavior of the system.
The output is not matching what we expected.

expressions.usage: "as ~ as possible" 구문으로 최대한의 정도를 나타낸다
name을 동사로 써서 "구체적으로 지칭하라"는 의미를 전달한다

patterns.pattern: Instead, be as [adjective] as possible: [imperative with list]
patterns.explanation: 앞의 금지 조언에 이어 대안을 명령문으로 제시하고, 콜론 뒤에 구체적 행동을 나열하는 패턴.
examples: Instead, be as clear as possible: state your name, your issue, and your preferred outcome.
Instead, be as direct as possible: mention the deadline, the budget, and the key requirement.

drillQuestions.question: Be as _____ as possible when describing a problem.
"Name the system" means to _____ which system has the problem.
The sentence lists four things to name: system, step, number, and _____.
"Is not matching what you expected" means the result is _____.

sentences.notes: 앞의 금지 조언(Avoid ~)에 대한 대안을 제시하는 문장. 두 개의 명령문(be specific / name ~)으로 실행 가능한 행동을 구체적으로 안내한다. 네 가지 병렬 목적어가 구체성의 범위를 넓힌다.

koreanAlignment.en: Instead, be as specific as possible
name the system, the step, the number
or the behavior that is not matching
what you expected

pronunciationNotes: specific: /spəˈsɪfɪk/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "스퍼시픽"에 가깝다.
behavior: /bɪˈheɪvjər/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "비헤이비어"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Visual aids — a screenshot, a simple diagram, or even a quick sketch on paper — can often communicate in seconds what words take minutes to explain.
sentences.translation: 시각 자료 — 스크린샷, 간단한 다이어그램, 또는 종이 위의 빠른 스케치라도 — 말로 설명하는 데 몇 분이 걸리는 것을 몇 초 만에 전달할 수 있다.

grammarElements.text: a screenshot, a simple diagram, or even a quick sketch on paper
grammarElements.label: 대시 삽입 동격어구 (예시 나열)

grammarElements.text: what words take minutes to explain
grammarElements.label: 간접의문문 (목적어)

vocabulary.example: Visual aids make presentations more effective.
Draw a simple diagram to show the process.
Images can communicate ideas faster than words.

expressions.usage: 매우 짧은 시간 내에 이루어짐을 강조하는 시간 표현
예상보다 단순한 것까지 포함됨을 나타내는 강조 표현

patterns.pattern: [Tool] — [example A], [example B], or even [example C] — can [verb] in [short time] what [alternative] takes [long time] to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 대시 삽입으로 예시를 나열하고, 시간 대비를 통해 도구의 효율성을 강조하는 패턴.
examples: Digital tools — a spreadsheet, a graph, or even a simple table — can summarize in moments what reports take hours to compile.
Maps — a satellite view, a street map, or even a rough drawing — can show in seconds what directions take paragraphs to describe.

drillQuestions.question: Visual aids can _____ communicate what words cannot.
A screenshot, a diagram, or even a quick _____ on paper can help.
"What words take minutes to explain" contrasts with "in _____."
"Visual aids" are tools that help through _____.

sentences.notes: 시각 자료의 효과를 "seconds vs. minutes" 시간 대비로 강조하는 문장. 대시 삽입(— a screenshot, a simple diagram, or even a quick sketch —)이 시각 자료의 구체적 예를 제공한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Visual aids
a screenshot, a simple diagram, or even a quick sketch on paper
koreanAlignment.ko: 스크린샷, 간단한 다이어그램, 또는 종이 위의 빠른 스케치라도

koreanAlignment.en: can often communicate in seconds
koreanAlignment.ko: 몇 초 만에 전달할 수 있다

koreanAlignment.en: what words take minutes to explain
koreanAlignment.ko: 말로 설명하는 데 몇 분이 걸리는 것을

pronunciationNotes: visual: /ˈvɪʒuəl/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "비주얼"에 가깝다. s가 /ʒ/로 발음된다.
diagram: /ˈdaɪəɡræm/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "다이어그램"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Finally, end with a clear question or request: "Can you check whether the formula changed?" is far more helpful than "What do you think?"
sentences.translation: 마지막으로, 명확한 질문이나 요청으로 마무리하라: "공식이 바뀌었는지 확인해 주시겠어요?"가 "어떻게 생각하세요?"보다 훨씬 더 도움이 된다.

grammarElements.text: end with a clear question or request
grammarElements.label: 명령문

grammarElements.text: "Can you check whether the formula changed?"
grammarElements.label: 인용문 주어 (좋은 예)

vocabulary.example: End your explanation with a clear request.
Check whether the formula was updated.
A specific question is more helpful than a vague one.

expressions.usage: 마지막 단계에서 해야 할 행동을 안내하는 표현
far가 비교급을 강조하여 차이가 큼을 나타낸다

patterns.pattern: Finally, [imperative]: "[good example]" is far more [adjective] than "[bad example]"
patterns.explanation: 마지막 조언을 명령문으로 제시하고, 콜론 뒤에 좋은 예와 나쁜 예를 비교급으로 대비하는 패턴.
examples: Finally, close with a specific ask: "Can we meet Tuesday?" is far more productive than "Let me know."
Finally, summarize the key point: "The server needs a restart" is far more useful than "There is a problem."

drillQuestions.question: _____, end with a clear question or request.
A specific question is far more _____ than a vague one.
"Can you check whether the formula changed?" is a _____ question.
"Far more helpful" uses "far" to _____ the comparison.

sentences.notes: 설명 과정의 마지막 단계를 안내하는 문장. "Finally"로 순서의 종결을 알리고, 두 인용문의 대비("Can you check ~" vs. "What do you think?")로 구체적 요청의 가치를 보여 준다.

koreanAlignment.en: Finally, end with a clear question or request
"Can you check whether the formula changed?"
is far more helpful than
"What do you think?"

pronunciationNotes: finally: /ˈfaɪnəli/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "파이널리"에 가깝다.
formula: /ˈfɔːrmjələ/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "포뮬러"에 가깝다. 복수형은 formulas 또는 formulae.

sentences.original: The goal is not to sound impressive but to make it as easy as possible for the other person to help you.
sentences.translation: 목표는 인상적으로 들리는 것이 아니라, 상대방이 당신을 돕는 것을 가능한 한 쉽게 만드는 것이다.

grammarElements.text: but to make it as easy as possible
grammarElements.label: 보어 (대조 to 부정사 + as ~ as possible)

grammarElements.text: for the other person to help you
grammarElements.label: for + 의미상 주어 + to 부정사

vocabulary.example: Do not try to sound impressive; be clear instead.
The goal is clarity, not complexity.
Make it as easy as possible for others.

expressions.phrase: not to A but to B
expressions.usage: 진짜 목적을 밝히기 위해 잘못된 목적을 먼저 부정하는 대조 구문
"as ~ as possible" 구문으로 최대한의 용이함을 표현한다

patterns.pattern: The goal is not to [false purpose] but to [true purpose]
patterns.explanation: 글의 핵심 논지를 "not A but B" 구조로 명확히 구분하는 마무리 패턴.
examples: The goal is not to write the most but to write the best.
The goal is not to avoid mistakes but to learn from them quickly.

drillQuestions.question: The goal is _____ to sound impressive but to be clear.
Make it as _____ as possible for the other person.
"To sound impressive" means to try to _____ others with your words.
"For the other person to help you" shows who the explanation is _____.

sentences.notes: "not to ~ but to ~" 구조가 글의 핵심 메시지(명확함이 목적이지 인상적으로 들리는 것이 목적이 아님)를 요약한다. "as easy as possible"이 청자 중심의 소통 철학을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The goal is not to sound impressive
but to make it as easy as possible
for the other person to help you

pronunciationNotes: impressive: /ɪmˈpresɪv/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "임프레시브"에 가깝다.
possible: /ˈpɑːsəbl/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "파서블"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Good communicators are not the ones who use the most words — they are the ones who use the fewest words to get the right idea across.
sentences.translation: 좋은 소통가는 가장 많은 말을 하는 사람이 아니라 — 가장 적은 말로 올바른 생각을 전달하는 사람이다.

grammarElements.text: are not the ones who use the most words
grammarElements.label: 보어 (부정 + 관계절)

grammarElements.text: they are the ones who use the fewest words
grammarElements.label: 대시 뒤 대조절 (관계절)

grammarElements.text: to get the right idea across
grammarElements.label: to 부정사 (목적)

vocabulary.example: The best communicators keep their messages short.
Use the fewest words possible to make your point.
She got her idea across very clearly.

expressions.usage: 자신의 의도나 생각을 상대방에게 이해시키다는 의미의 구동사

expressions.phrase: not the ones who ... they are the ones who
expressions.usage: 두 관계절을 대비하여 진짜 속성을 강조하는 정의 구문

patterns.pattern: [Subject] are not the ones who [A] — they are the ones who [B]
patterns.explanation: 대시(—)를 사이에 두고 부정(not the ones who)과 긍정(they are the ones who)을 대비하여 핵심 정의를 내리는 마무리 패턴.
examples: Great leaders are not the ones who talk the loudest — they are the ones who listen the most.
True experts are not the ones who know everything — they are the ones who know what matters.

drillQuestions.question: Good communicators use the _____ words to get the idea across.
"Get the right idea across" means to _____ the message successfully.
"The ones who" introduces a _____ clause.
This sentence defines what makes a _____ communicator.

sentences.notes: 글 전체의 결론이자 핵심 교훈을 격언 형태로 요약하는 문장. "not the ones who ~ they are the ones who ~" 대비 구조가 간결함의 가치를 선명하게 드러내며, 독자에게 기억에 남는 마무리를 제공한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Good communicators are not the ones who use the most words
koreanAlignment.ko: 좋은 소통가는 가장 많은 말을 하는 사람이 아니라

koreanAlignment.en: they are the ones who use the fewest words
to get the right idea across

pronunciationNotes: communicators: /kəˈmjuːnɪkeɪtərz/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "커뮤니케이터즈"에 가깝다.
fewest: /ˈfjuːɪst/ — 한 음절에 가깝게 발음. "퓨이스트"에 가깝다. few의 최상급.
